nativeRange.habitat: Adults generally inhabit coastal to outer reefs, ranging from silty lagoons to pristine outer reef walls, at depths reported from 0 to 80 m.

diet.notes: Has been documented feeding on a wide variety of benthic invertebrates in the wild — including molluscs, crustaceans, echinoderms, and worms. In captivity tends to accept meaty foods such as squid, shrimp, krill, and clams; hard-shelled prey is generally advised to help wear down the continuously growing teeth.

avoidWith: anything small enough to be eaten

summary: A mid-sized Indo-Pacific triggerfish recognized by the distinctive half-moon marking at the tail base and yellow-tipped fins. Generally considered hardy and easy to feed, but widely regarded as aggressive and incompatible with reef tanks or invertebrates.
careNotes:
The White Tip Triggerfish (also commonly traded as the Halfmoon Triggerfish) is generally considered a hardy species that tends to acclimate readily to aquarium life. It is best kept in a tank of at least 300 L to accommodate its active, territorial nature. A tight-fitting lid is advisable, as triggerfish are known to be strong enough to dislodge equipment and have been observed interacting with tank covers.

The White Tip Triggerfish is not reef compatible. It has been known to eat invertebrates including crustaceans, molluscs, and echinoderms, and will generally target corals in a feeding context as well. It is best kept alongside other large, robust fish of similar temperament; aggression toward conspecifics and similar species is commonly reported.

The species has been described as solitary and territorial in the wild, which is consistent with aquarium reports of aggression toward tankmates perceived as competitors. Introducing it last into an established tank is commonly advised to reduce territorial behavior.

Like other triggerfishes, the White Tip Triggerfish has a dorsal locking-spine mechanism — a rigid spine it can lock upright to wedge itself into a crevice or deter a threat. This spine may be used defensively when the fish feels threatened, so handling should be done with care.
breedingNotes: Breeding in captivity has not been documented for this species. In the wild, the species is oviparous (egg-laying) and has been reported to pair monogamously. It has not been bred in home aquaria.

primary.name: BlueZoo Aquatics
primary.notes: Used as effective primary source; LiveAquaria (assigned primary) does not currently list this species. BlueZoo provided: adult size (12 in / ~30 cm), care level (beginner), temperament (aggressive), minimum tank (75 gal), diet (carnivore), reef safe (no), and native range (Indo-Pacific, Africa, Fiji).

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (30.0 cm TL — consistent with BlueZoo 30.5 cm, no disagreement), temperature range (24.8–29.3°C), native range (East Africa through northern Australia to Samoa; north to southern Japan), taxonomy (Balistidae/Tetraodontiformes), habitat (coastal to outer reefs, 0–80 m depth), and behavior (solitary, territorial, oviparous/monogamous).

additional.name: LiveAquaria
additional.notes: Assigned primary source; species not found in current collection listing or search results. Swapped with BlueZoo Aquatics as effective primary. LiveAquaria triggerfish collection page confirms general care context for family Balistidae.

fish.breedingNotes: Not bred in captivity. Wild reproduction is oviparous, and monogamous pairing behavior has been reported.
